title: Blood — what's in it and what it does
examTip: AQA tests all four blood components every year. For each one you need its structure, function, and any adaptations that make it suited to its job.
introTips: This lesson breaks blood into its four components — plasma, red blood cells, white blood cells, and platelets — and explains exactly what each one does.
For red blood cells, you'll need to explain two structural adaptations and link them to their function — this is a classic AQA question.
By the end you'll be able to write a detailed answer on any blood component, including the adaptation → function link AQA loves to test.
intro: Blood looks like a simple red liquid, but it's actually a mixture of four very different things doing four very different jobs. Spin it in a centrifuge and it separates into distinct layers — and each layer tells a story about how your body keeps itself alive.

keyPoints.heading: Plasma — the liquid carrier
keyPoints.content: Plasma is the straw-coloured liquid that makes up about 55% of blood. Everything else travels dissolved in it. Plasma carries digested food molecules (glucose, amino acids, fatty acids) from the small intestine to cells that need them. It also carries hormones like insulin, carbon dioxide from respiring cells to the lungs, urea from the liver to the kidneys, and antibodies. It also distributes heat around the body.
writeBullets: Carries: glucose, amino acids, fatty acids (from gut to cells)
Carries: hormones (e.g. insulin), CO₂, urea, antibodies
Also distributes heat around the body

keyTerms.def: The liquid component of blood; carries dissolved substances including glucose, hormones, CO₂, urea and antibodies

keyPoints.heading: Red blood cells — oxygen delivery
keyPoints.content: Red blood cells have two features that make them brilliant at carrying oxygen. First, they have no nucleus — that space is used for more haemoglobin, the protein that actually binds the oxygen. Second, they have a biconcave disc shape — like a squashed doughnut — which gives a large surface area relative to their volume, so oxygen can diffuse in and out quickly. In the lungs, haemoglobin picks up oxygen to form oxyhaemoglobin. In the tissues, it releases it, becoming deoxyhaemoglobin again.
writeBullets: No nucleus → more room for haemoglobin
Biconcave disc shape → large surface area for faster diffusion of O₂
Made in red bone marrow · live ~120 days

keyTerms.def: The iron-containing protein in red blood cells that binds oxygen in the lungs and releases it in the tissues
Haemoglobin combined with oxygen; formed in the lungs and broken down in the tissues

keyPoints.heading: White blood cells — your immune defence
keyPoints.content: White blood cells are part of the immune system. Unlike red blood cells, they all have a nucleus. There are two main types. Phagocytes destroy pathogens by engulfing and digesting them — this is called phagocytosis. Lymphocytes produce antibodies — proteins specifically shaped to lock on to a particular pathogen and neutralise it. Lymphocytes also produce antitoxins to counteract toxins released by bacteria. White blood cells are far fewer in number than red blood cells, but they're essential.
writeBullets: White blood cells: have a nucleus (unlike RBCs)
Lymphocytes: produce antibodies specific to each pathogen
Lymphocytes also produce antitoxins to counteract bacterial toxins
Fewer in number than RBCs but vital for immunity

keyTerms.def: A type of white blood cell that engulfs and digests pathogens
A type of white blood cell that produces antibodies specific to a particular pathogen
A protein produced by lymphocytes that binds specifically to an antigen on a pathogen, neutralising it

keyPoints.heading: Platelets — sealing wounds
keyPoints.content: Platelets are small fragments of cells — they have no nucleus. When you cut yourself, platelets clump together at the wound and trigger a series of chemical reactions that form a clot. The clot seals the wound, stops blood loss, and prevents pathogens getting in. This is called blood clotting. Without enough platelets, even a small cut could be dangerous.
writeBullets: Clot: stops blood loss + prevents pathogen entry
Too few platelets = dangerous bleeding risk

keyTerms.def: A cell fragment with no nucleus that triggers blood clotting at wound sites

tables.title: The four components of blood
rows: Carries dissolved substances: glucose, hormones, CO₂, urea, antibodies, heat
Biconcave disc; no nucleus; contains haemoglobin
Carry oxygen from lungs to tissues (as oxyhaemoglobin)
Phagocytes digest pathogens; lymphocytes make antibodies
Form clots to seal wounds and prevent blood loss

1.question: Which substance in red blood cells actually carries oxygen?
1.correctFeedback: Correct — haemoglobin is the iron-containing protein that binds oxygen in the lungs and releases it in the tissues.
1.wrongFeedback: It's haemoglobin — the iron-containing protein inside red blood cells. Plasma carries dissolved substances but not oxygen. Antibodies are made by lymphocytes. Red blood cells have no nucleus.

3.question: What do lymphocytes produce to fight infection?
3.correctFeedback: Right — lymphocytes produce antibodies, each shaped to match a specific pathogen. They also produce antitoxins.
3.wrongFeedback: Lymphocytes produce antibodies — proteins specifically shaped to lock on to a particular pathogen. Haemoglobin is in red blood cells, platelets are cell fragments, and plasma is the liquid carrier.

commonMistakes: Saying red blood cells have a nucleus — they don't. That's the whole point; losing the nucleus makes room for more haemoglobin.
Confusing phagocytes and lymphocytes — phagocytes EAT pathogens, lymphocytes MAKE antibodies. Different jobs.
Describing platelets as cells — they're cell fragments (pieces of cells), not whole cells, and they have no nucleus.
Saying plasma carries oxygen — it doesn't. Oxygen is carried by haemoglobin in red blood cells. Plasma carries CO₂, glucose, hormones, urea.
examTips: For any 'explain how red blood cells are adapted' question, always give structure → function: 'no nucleus, so more room for haemoglobin, so more oxygen can be carried'; 'biconcave shape gives large surface area, so oxygen diffuses faster'.
If asked about white blood cells, name the type: don't just say 'white blood cells make antibodies' — say 'lymphocytes produce antibodies'.
The equation haemoglobin + oxygen → oxyhaemoglobin is reversible. In lungs it goes right. In tissues it goes left. Say which direction and why.
revisionCardBullets: Plasma (~55%): liquid; carries glucose, amino acids, hormones, CO₂, urea, antibodies, heat
Red blood cells: biconcave disc, no nucleus → more haemoglobin → more O₂ carried
Haemoglobin + O₂ → oxyhaemoglobin (lungs); releases O₂ in tissues
White blood cells: have nucleus; phagocytes engulf pathogens; lymphocytes make antibodies
Antibodies: specific proteins made by lymphocytes; shaped to match one pathogen
Antitoxins: produced by lymphocytes to counteract bacterial toxins
Platelets: cell fragments, no nucleus; trigger clotting to seal wounds
Adaptation → function: no nucleus in RBCs = more room for haemoglobin
Adaptation → function: biconcave shape = large surface area for O₂ diffusion
